af_score_components.notes: MCP server for Home Assistant smart home automation (quality 70). Bearer token auth — straightforward for agents. Controls devices, scenes, scripts, and automations. Rate limits from HA instance — varies by hardware. Requires local Home Assistant instance to be running. Device state queries may need polling — not all state changes are synchronous. Entity IDs required for all device operations — must discover. Smart home actions (lights, locks, thermostats) have real-world consequences. Good for home automation agents.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS only if Home Assistant is properly configured with SSL — often HTTP locally. Bearer token auth — should be long-lived access tokens, not user passwords. Limited scope granularity — same token controls all HA devices. Community/HA-maintained MCP server. Bearer token via environment variable. Smart home control (locks, alarms) carries physical security implications — treat with care. Local network exposure if HA is not externally accessible.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Self-hosted HA instance — uptime depends on local hardware. Version tied to Home Assistant release cadence. Breaking changes possible as HA API evolves. Device unavailability (offline devices) causes partial failures. Smart home operations typically idempotent (setting state is safe to retry). Local network required — no remote access without VPN or Nabu Casa. HA must be running for MCP server to operate.

af_score_components.notes: No MCP server. Honeycomb observability platform — OpenTelemetry traces, events, columns, and queries. Simple API key. Derived columns, SLOs, triggers, and board management via API. Query API for running analytics over trace data. Rate limits documented. Results endpoints for query output. Excellent for observability automation agents — monitoring SLOs, triggering alerts, analyzing trace data programmatically. Dataset-scoped operations require knowing dataset names.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. API key with team-level scoping. Limited granularity — same key accesses all datasets. Honeycomb maintained. SOC2/GDPR compliance. API key via environment variable. Trace data may contain sensitive application internals (request parameters, user IDs) — review data before agent analysis. Configuration API (triggers, SLOs) can affect production alerting — use carefully.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: SaaS with SLA and status page. Stable versioned API. Good migration guides. Retry guidance documented. Query results are async — must poll for completion. Trigger and SLO management idempotent with PUT. Rate limits on query endpoints — complex analytics queries consume more quota. Dataset retention limits — historical data may be unavailable beyond retention window.

af_score_components.notes: No MCP server. Hotjar behavioral analytics — heatmaps, session recordings, feedback surveys. OAuth2 only — no API key option for agents. Limited API surface — primarily read-only access to analytics data. Heatmap and recording data not fully accessible via API (dashboard only). Surveys and feedback can be managed via API. Rate limits documented. Not ideal for agent integration given OAuth2-only auth and limited API surface. Better suited for human-facing analytics review.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. OAuth2 only — required for all API access. Limited scope granularity. Hotjar maintained. GDPR compliance for behavioral analytics. OAuth tokens via secure flow. Session recording data contains user behavior including potentially sensitive interactions — strict data governance required. GDPR consent collection required before recording users. No service account pattern for agent auth.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: SaaS with SLA. Stable API. Migration guides available. Retry guidance documented. OAuth token refresh required for long-running agents. Limited API coverage of Hotjar features reduces usefulness for agent workflows. Analytics data has processing delay — real-time data not available via API. Rate limits vary by plan.

af_score_components.notes: Local MCP server for HTTPRunner (HTTP API testing framework) (quality 55). No auth required — local operation. Runs HTTP test cases defined in YAML/JSON/Python format. No rate limits (local tool). Test execution results are structured. Good for automating API test execution from agent workflows. Requires test files to be pre-defined. Limited documentation beyond HTTPRunner's own docs. Useful for CI/CD agent integration with existing test suites.

security_score_components.security_notes: Local operation — no network exposure for the MCP server itself. HTTP test execution may call external APIs — those must be secured per their requirements. No credentials stored in MCP server. Unknown dependency hygiene. Test credentials in test files must be managed carefully — avoid hardcoding sensitive values in test fixtures. Read-only server interaction from MCP perspective.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Self-hosted local tool with no SLA. Unknown version. No documented breaking change policy. Test execution idempotent for read tests; write tests may not be. External API availability affects test results. HTTPRunner version compatibility with MCP server must be maintained. Test file format changes may break existing tests.

af_score_components.notes: Official HubSpot MCP server (quality 78). Comprehensive CRM — contacts, companies, deals, tickets, workflows, emails. API key (private apps) or OAuth2. Rate limits documented (100 req/10sec on free, higher on paid). Property schema must be discovered before use. Batch operations available for efficiency. Workflows can be triggered via API. Association management for CRM object relationships. Excellent for CRM automation agents. v3 API preferred for new integrations.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. Private app tokens (API key pattern) or OAuth2. Private apps have scopes but limited granularity. HubSpot maintained. SOC2/GDPR compliance for CRM platform. API key or OAuth token via environment variable. CRM data contains customer PII — GDPR compliance required for EU customers. Private app scopes enable read-only access for monitoring agents. Webhook HMAC verification for event security.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Enterprise SaaS with SLA and status page. Stable versioned API. Excellent migration guides (v1/v2 to v3). Retry guidance documented. Rate limits vary by plan — free tier 100/10sec constrains bulk operations. Batch API reduces rate limit pressure for high-volume operations. Object IDs required for updates — discovery at setup. Association API complexity for linked CRM objects. Workflow triggers are async.

af_score_components.notes: No MCP server. HuggingFace Inference API and Hub API — model downloads, inference, dataset access, space management. Simple API key (HF token). Inference API for 100k+ hosted models. Rate limits vary by model and plan. Cold starts on free tier. PRO plan for dedicated inference. Model IDs required (e.g., 'meta-llama/Llama-3.2-1B'). Async inference for large models. Hub API for managing repos, datasets, files. Excellent for ML-centric agent workflows.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. HF token with read/write/admin scopes. Limited fine-grained permissions. HuggingFace maintained. GDPR compliance. HF_TOKEN via environment variable. Inference requests sent to HuggingFace servers — review data retention for sensitive prompts. Private models require write scope to access. Gated models require explicit access request approval. No formal compliance certifications beyond GDPR listed.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: SaaS with status page. Stable API versioning. Good migration guides. Retry guidance documented. Free tier has cold starts (30-60 sec) — use Serverless Inference API for warmer models. Model availability changes as HuggingFace updates their hosted models. Rate limits vary widely by model popularity. Private/gated models require pre-approval. Dedicated endpoints available for production reliability.

af_score_components.notes: No MCP server. Hunter.io email finding and verification API. Simple API key. Domain search, email finder, email verifier. Free tier: 25 searches/month — very restrictive. Confidence scores for email validity. Verification uses SMTP check — not 100% accurate. Domain search returns pattern + employees. Bulk verification available on paid plans. Rate limits documented. Good for sales and outreach agent workflows. GDPR compliance important — email discovery may require consent basis.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. Simple API key — no scope granularity. Hunter maintained. GDPR compliance for email data processing. API key via environment variable. Email finding and verification involves processing personal contact data — GDPR legitimate interest basis required. Email lists built via Hunter must have appropriate legal basis for contact. Read-only data collection operations reduce write-side risk.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: SaaS with status page. Stable API versioning. Good backward compatibility. Retry guidance documented. Free tier (25 searches/month) creates hard monthly availability boundary. Verification accuracy depends on target mail server response — some servers block SMTP checks. Domain search results update as Hunter crawls new sources. Email confidence scores are probabilistic — not guaranteed accurate.

af_score_components.notes: MCP server with multiple auth options (quality 78): basic auth, token, and keyring integration. Flexible auth supports various deployment scenarios. Keyring integration enables secure OS-level credential storage. Rate limits depend on underlying resources. Good documentation for auth configuration. Tool coverage depends on installed plugins/extensions. Auth flexibility makes it adaptable to enterprise environments. Good for teams with strict credential management requirements.

security_score_components.security_notes: TLS enforced for external connections. Multiple auth options: basic (weakest), token, keyring (strongest). Keyring integration uses OS secure credential storage — excellent for production. Auth scope granularity depends on configuration. Unknown dependency hygiene. Keyring integration prevents credentials from appearing in environment variables or config files. Token-based auth recommended over basic for production deployments.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Self-hosted with no SLA. Moderate version stability. No formal breaking change policy. Reliability depends on configured plugins and backends. Keyring availability varies by OS and configuration. Auth method selection affects error recovery (keyring failures vs token expiry). Good foundation for building reliable local tool infrastructure.

af_score_components.notes: Local MCP server for IDA Pro (commercial reverse engineering/disassembler tool) (quality 72). No auth required — IDA Pro plugin. Enables LLM-assisted binary analysis: disassembly, decompilation (Hexrays), cross-reference navigation, function renaming. IDA Pro license required (expensive commercial tool). IDA Pro must be running with plugin loaded. Very high value for security research and malware analysis workflows. Hexrays decompiler produces high-quality pseudocode. Excellent for professional security research agents.

security_score_components.security_notes: Local IDA Pro plugin — no network exposure. No credentials required or stored. Read-only binary analysis operations primarily. Community-maintained plugin — dependency hygiene depends on contributors. Analyzing malware requires air-gapped or sandboxed environment. Never analyze untrusted binaries on production systems with network access. IDA Pro license key is the primary credential to protect. Local access controls provide implicit security.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Self-hosted — IDA Pro must be running with plugin loaded. Version tied to IDA Pro API compatibility. No documented breaking change policy. Binary analysis queries idempotent. IDA Pro crashes can require restart — no automatic recovery. Large binary analysis consumes significant memory. Plugin must be updated when IDA Pro updates break API compatibility. High value for specialized security workflows despite reliability constraints.

af_score_components.notes: No MCP server. ImageKit image optimization, transformation, and CDN. API key for upload and management; URL signature for secure transformations. Real-time image transformations via URL parameters (resize, format, quality). Media library management. Transformations are cached — first request may be slower. Rate limits documented by plan. Good multi-language SDK. Excellent for media-heavy agent workflows requiring dynamic image optimization.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. API key + private key pair for authentication. URL signature with private key for secure transformations. ImageKit maintained. No formal compliance certifications listed. API key and private key via environment variables. URL signatures prevent unauthorized transformations. Public vs private endpoint distinction — private requires signed URLs. No fine-grained permission scoping.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: CDN SaaS with SLA and global status. Stable versioned API. Good migration guides. Retry guidance documented. CDN caching makes repeated transformation requests fast. Cache invalidation required when replacing images. Upload operations not idempotent — same filename may be overwritten. Rate limits by plan — monitor for high-volume media operations. Global CDN provides reliable image delivery.
